Name: LatencyAwareAdaptiveMixedPrecision
Title: Latency-Aware Adaptive Mixed Precision for Efficient Inference in Vision Transformers
Short Hypothesis: Developing a latency-aware adaptive mixed-precision (LAMP) framework for vision transformers (ViTs) that dynamically adjusts precision levels based on real-time inference constraints, while maintaining high accuracy and reducing computational overhead.
Related Work: Existing work in adaptive mixed-precision (AMP) for vision transformers (ViTs) has focused on balancing accuracy and efficiency through post-training quantization [1, 2] and training-free proxies [3]. However, these methods often overlook the latency constraints critical for real-time applications. Our proposal introduces a latency-aware framework that dynamically adjusts precision levels during inference, ensuring that the model meets strict latency requirements while maintaining high accuracy.
Abstract: Vision transformers (ViTs) have revolutionized computer vision but face significant challenges in terms of computational and memory demands, especially in real-time applications. This proposal introduces Latency-Aware Adaptive Mixed Precision (LAMP), a framework that dynamically adjusts precision levels during inference to meet strict latency constraints while maintaining high accuracy. LAMP leverages a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism and uncertainty-quantified activation proxies to minimize hidden-state perturbation and enforce strict wall-clock budgets. We validate LAMP through a series of experiments on ViT, DeiT, and Swin models, demonstrating significant improvements in latency-accuracy trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Baseline Performance Evaluation - Evaluate the baseline performance of ViT, DeiT, and Swin models on the ImageNet dataset using full-precision inference. Record accuracy and inference latency.

Experiment 2: Latency-Aware Precision Adjustment - Implement the LAMP framework and dynamically adjust precision levels during inference. Use Jacobian-based layer-skipping to identify critical layers for high precision and less critical layers for lower precision. Measure the impact on inference latency and accuracy.

Experiment 3: Uncertainty-Quantified Activation Proxies - Introduce uncertainty-quantified activation proxies to dynamically adjust precision levels based on real-time inference constraints. Evaluate the effectiveness of these proxies in maintaining high accuracy while reducing computational overhead.

Experiment 4: Comparative Analysis - Compare LAMP with state-of-the-art adaptive mixed-precision methods (e.g., AMP-ViT, SNIP) in terms of latency, accuracy, and computational efficiency. Use metrics such as throughput, FLOPs, and memory usage.

Experiment 5: Robustness Evaluation - Test the robustness of LAMP under structured perturbations and domain shifts. Evaluate the stability of the model in different environments and quantify the impact on latency and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Overhead of Jacobian Calculation - The computational overhead of calculating Jacobians in real-time could impact the overall latency. We will mitigate this by using efficient approximation techniques and amortizing the cost over multiple layers.

Risk 2: Sensitivity to Initial Precision Settings - The initial precision settings could significantly affect the performance of the LAMP framework. We will conduct extensive hyperparameter tuning to identify optimal initial settings.
Limitation 1: Limited to Vision Transformers - The proposed framework is primarily designed for vision transformers and may not be directly applicable to other types of models.

Limitation 2: Dependence on Hardware - The effectiveness of LAMP may vary depending on the hardware used for inference. We will test the framework on multiple GPU platforms to ensure generalizability.
